mappings.rationaleAI: Directly addresses the crusading movement from First through later Crusades (A.1.a, A.1.b, A.1.d), including Urban II at Clermont (A.1.a.i) and Byzantine interactions (A.1.c).
Byzantine history covers the Comnenus dynasty (A.1.c), Alexius I and the First Crusade (A.1.c.i), later Comneni (A.1.c.ii), the Fourth Crusade (A.1.c.iii), and the Palaeologus dynasty (A.3).
Mongol campaigns address the invasion of Rus (A.1.e), invasions of eastern Europe and Southwest Asia (B.2), and successor states (B.3.a).
Arabs, Persians, and Turks in Islamic civilization connect to Islamic states of Southwest Asia (B.1) and North African developments (B.4).
Sunni-Shia divisions relate to Seljuq Sunni supremacy (B.1.a.ii) and Mamluk orthodoxy (B.4.b.ii). The Ismaili community relates to the Assassins (B.1.b).
Muscovite state formation under Tatar rule connects to Russia under Mongol invasion (A.1.e) and rise of Moscow (A.2.a).
The Great Schism and Orthodox-Byzantine relationship connect to A.1.c and the Palaeologus cultural revival (A.3.b).
Iranian civilization connects to Seljuq establishment in Iran (B.1.a) and Il-Khan conversion to Islam (B.3.a.i).
Dynastic succession connects to Seljuq Empire (B.1), Ottoman state (B.3.b), and Ayyubid/Mamluk dynasties (B.4.b).
Philosophical traditions connect to late medieval North African intellectual life including Sufism (B.4.d.i).
Religious justification of war connects to the crusading movement (A.1) and Berber religious reform movements (B.4.c).
Disruption of pilgrimage routes relates to the background of the First Crusade (A.1.a.i).
Relevant to the spread and evolution of Islam across North Africa, Southwest Asia, and Anatolia discussed in subsection B.
